metadata.title: Build a Self-Critique Prompt Checker with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Hola, Liam — in for Bear. How does build a self-critique prompt checker with claude? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Build a Self-Critique Prompt Checker with Claude?
props.segment: Build a Self-Critique Prompt Checker with

beats.label: Hook — vague vs structured critique
beats.narration: Claude is reasonably good at critiquing outputs when given specific criteria. But critique quality degrades dramatically without structured guidance — and most people use the vague version.

shots.notes: Two critique prompts side by side. LEFT: 'How could this be better?' -> generic output: 'Could be clearer, more concise...' CRIMSON label: VAGUE. RIGHT: 'Critique against: [3 specific criteria]. Pass/fail + evidence per criterion.' -> structured output with pass/fail rows. GREEN label: STRUCTURED.

beats.label: Claude command
beats.narration: The structured critique command cites the specific criteria from the original prompt, requires pass/fail with one-sentence evidence, and lists the top 3 changes needed — without making the changes yet.

props.command: claude "Critique this output against these specific criteria: [list from original prompt]. For each criterion: pass/fail with one-sentence evidence. Then list top 3 changes. Do not make changes yet — only critique." < output.md

beats.label: Python code
beats.narration: The script runs three stages: generate initial output, structured critique against criteria, then revised output incorporating the top-3 changes. All three appear in the terminal.

props.code:
import subprocess, sys
prompt = open(sys.argv[1]).read()
criteria = open(sys.argv[2]).read()
initial = subprocess.run(
  ["claude", prompt], capture_output=True, text=True
).stdout
critique_cmd = (
  f"Critique against: {criteria}. "
  "Pass/fail + evidence per criterion. "
  "Top 3 changes. Do not revise yet."
)
critique = subprocess.run(
  ["claude", critique_cmd], input=initial,
  capture_output=True, text=True
).stdout
print("CRITIQUE:\n", critique)

beats.label: Critique pass/fail
beats.narration: The critique shows three criteria with pass/fail verdicts and one-sentence evidence from the output — not generic feedback, but specific cites from the text.

shots.notes: Three-row critique table. Column headers: CRITERION | VERDICT | EVIDENCE. Row 1: Word count under 100 | PASS (green) | 'Output is 87 words.' Row 2: No hedging language | FAIL (crimson) | 'Sentence 3: could potentially...' Row 3: Three bullet points | FAIL (crimson) | 'Output uses prose, not bullets.' Bottom: 'Top 3 changes: 1. Remove hedge in S3. 2. Convert to bullets. 3. ...'

beats.label: The change — vague critique
beats.narration: Now run the same loop with a vague self-critique prompt. Watch the critique quality degrade — no criteria, no evidence, generic suggestions.

props.command: claude "How could this be better?" < output.md

beats.label: Vague critique output
beats.narration: The vague critique output: 'Could be clearer. More concise might help. Consider adding examples.' No criteria. No evidence. No specific change target. Unusable for revision.

shots.notes: VAGUE CRITIQUE output block: 'Could be clearer. More concise might help. Consider adding examples.' CRIMSON annotation: 'No criteria. No evidence. No change target.' Teardown palette, CRIMSON border.

beats.label: Teardown angle
beats.narration: Self-critique is only useful when the critique criteria are as specific as the original task criteria. Otherwise you're asking Claude to evaluate against its own taste — which is just more of the same.

shots.notes: Large text: 'Specific criteria → inspectable critique.' Below: 'Vague prompt → Claude evaluates against its own taste.' CRIMSON rule. Two-line contrast. Cream background.
